sections.heading: What is RecyclerView?
sections.content: RecyclerView is Android's most powerful and flexible widget for displaying large scrollable lists and grids of data. It is the modern replacement for the older ListView and GridView. The name 'RecyclerView' comes from its core optimization: instead of creating a new View for every item in the list, it recycles off-screen item Views and rebinds them with new data as the user scrolls. This makes it extremely memory-efficient and smooth even with thousands of items.

sections.heading: Why RecyclerView Over ListView?
items: View recycling is mandatory and built-in — no need for the ViewHolder pattern workaround
Supports multiple layout managers: vertical list, horizontal list, grid, staggered grid
Built-in support for item animations — add, remove, and move animations out of the box
DiffUtil support for efficient, animated list updates without full redraws
Supports item decorations for dividers, spacing, and custom drawing between items
Supports multiple view types in the same list (e.g., header + items + footer)
ItemTouchHelper provides drag-and-drop and swipe-to-dismiss with minimal code
ConcatAdapter allows combining multiple adapters for complex list structures

rows.role: The scrollable container View placed in your XML layout
Bridges your data source to the RecyclerView; creates and binds ViewHolders
Holds references to the Views for a single list item to avoid repeated findViewById calls
Determines how items are arranged: list, grid, or staggered grid
Adds visual decorations like dividers, spacing, or custom drawing around items
Handles animations for item add, remove, and change operations
Calculates the minimum set of changes between two lists for efficient updates
An Adapter subclass with built-in DiffUtil and submitList() for easy list updates

sections.heading: Setting Up RecyclerView
sections.content: To use RecyclerView, you first add the androidx.recyclerview dependency to your build.gradle.kts. Then you add the RecyclerView widget to your XML layout. In your Activity or Fragment, you create an Adapter, set a LayoutManager, and assign the adapter to the RecyclerView. The LayoutManager and Adapter are the two required components — without both, the RecyclerView will show nothing.

sections.heading: Steps to Implement RecyclerView
items: Step 1: Add RecyclerView dependency in build.gradle.kts: implementation("androidx.recyclerview:recyclerview:1.3.2")
Step 2: Add <RecyclerView> to your activity or fragment XML layout with an ID
Step 3: Create an item layout XML file (e.g., item_user.xml) for a single list row
Step 4: Create a data model class (e.g., data class User(val id: Int, val name: String))
Step 5: Create an Adapter class that extends ListAdapter<YourModel, YourViewHolder>
Step 6: Create a ViewHolder inner class that holds references to item Views using View Binding
Step 7: Implement onCreateViewHolder() to inflate the item layout and return a ViewHolder
Step 8: Implement onBindViewHolder() to bind data from your list to the ViewHolder's Views
Step 9: In your Activity/Fragment, set layoutManager and adapter on the RecyclerView

rows.use_case: Horizontally scrollable carousels or tag lists

rows.arrangement: Grid with fixed number of columns
rows.use_case: Photo galleries, product grids, icon grids

rows.arrangement: Grid where items can have different heights

sections.heading: The ViewHolder Pattern
sections.content: The ViewHolder pattern is the cornerstone of RecyclerView performance. A ViewHolder is a class that holds direct references to the Views inside a single list item layout. Without it, Android would have to call findViewById() every time an item is bound — which is expensive because it traverses the entire view tree. By storing references once in the ViewHolder at creation time, binding becomes a simple property assignment operation. Modern ViewHolders use View Binding to eliminate even the manual reference storage.

sections.heading: RecyclerView.Adapter — Required Methods
items: onCreateViewHolder(parent, viewType) – Called when RecyclerView needs a new ViewHolder; inflate your item layout here
onBindViewHolder(holder, position) – Called to bind data to a recycled ViewHolder; set text, images, and click listeners here
getItemCount() – Returns the total number of items in the list; RecyclerView won't show anything if this returns 0
getItemViewType(position) – Override to return different view types for different item positions (optional)
onViewRecycled(holder) – Called when a ViewHolder is recycled; useful for releasing resources like image loads
onViewAttachedToWindow(holder) – Called when a ViewHolder's View is added to the window
onViewDetachedFromWindow(holder) – Called when a ViewHolder's View is removed from the window

rows.list_adapter: Built-in — pass a DiffUtil.ItemCallback in the constructor
Call submitList(newList) — handles diffing and animation automatically
rows.recyclerview_adapter: Must call notifyDataSetChanged() or granular notify methods manually

rows.list_adapter: Automatic — adds, removes, and moves are animated

rows.recyclerview_adapter: Worse if using notifyDataSetChanged() — redraws all items

rows.list_adapter: Yes — use for almost all cases
rows.recyclerview_adapter: Only when you need very custom diffing logic

sections.heading: DiffUtil & ItemCallback
sections.content: DiffUtil is a utility class that calculates the difference between two lists using the Myers diff algorithm. It figures out which items were added, removed, moved, or changed and applies only the necessary updates to the RecyclerView with animations. When using ListAdapter, you provide a DiffUtil.ItemCallback that tells DiffUtil how to compare items. You must implement two methods: areItemsTheSame() to check identity (usually by ID) and areContentsTheSame() to check if the visual content has changed.

sections.heading: Implementing DiffUtil.ItemCallback
items: areItemsTheSame(oldItem, newItem) – Returns true if both items represent the same object (compare by unique ID, not content)
areContentsTheSame(oldItem, newItem) – Returns true if the items have identical visual content (compare all displayed fields)
getChangePayload(oldItem, newItem) – Optional; returns a payload object for partial bind updates (avoids full rebind)
With Kotlin data classes, areContentsTheSame can simply use oldItem == newItem (structural equality)
Never compare items by reference (===) in areContentsTheSame — this will always return false for new list instances
DiffUtil runs on the main thread in ListAdapter by default — for very large lists, use AsyncListDiffer
Provide stable, unique IDs for your items to help DiffUtil correctly identify moved items

rows.description: Redraws all visible items — no animations; avoid for performance
rows.when_to_use: Last resort when you cannot determine what changed

rows.description: Animates insertion of a single item at the given position
rows.when_to_use: When adding one item to a known position

rows.description: Animates removal of a single item from the given position
rows.when_to_use: When deleting one item from a known position

rows.description: Triggers rebind of a single item without animation
rows.when_to_use: When one item's data changes without structural list changes

rows.description: Animates movement of an item from one position to another
Animates insertion of multiple consecutive items
rows.when_to_use: When appending a page of results (pagination)

sections.heading: Handling Click Events in RecyclerView
items: Set click listeners inside onBindViewHolder() on the item root View or specific child Views
Pass a lambda callback from the Activity/Fragment to the Adapter constructor for click handling
Example: class UserAdapter(private val onItemClick: (User) -> Unit) : ListAdapter<User, ...>()
In onBindViewHolder: holder.itemView.setOnClickListener { onItemClick(getItem(position)) }
Use holder.bindingAdapterPosition instead of position in async callbacks to avoid stale positions
For long-click: holder.itemView.setOnLongClickListener { onItemLongClick(item); true }
Avoid creating new lambda objects in onBindViewHolder for every bind — define them at creation time where possible
Use sealed classes or interfaces for click event types when items have multiple clickable zones

rows.description: Built-in decoration that draws a line divider between items
Extend RecyclerView.ItemDecoration and override getItemOffsets() to add spacing around items
rows.usage: Override getItemOffsets(outRect, view, parent, state) and set outRect.bottom = 16 (in px)

rows.description: You can add multiple ItemDecorations to the same RecyclerView
rows.usage: Call addItemDecoration() multiple times with different decoration instances

sections.heading: Multiple View Types in RecyclerView
items: Override getItemViewType(position) in your Adapter to return different Int constants for different item types
Create separate ViewHolder classes for each view type (e.g., HeaderViewHolder, ItemViewHolder, FooterViewHolder)
In onCreateViewHolder(), use a when(viewType) block to inflate the correct layout for each type
In onBindViewHolder(), cast the holder to the appropriate type using when(holder) or is checks
Use sealed classes for your data model to represent different item types cleanly
ConcatAdapter is the modern approach: create separate adapters for header, list, and footer and concatenate them
Define view type constants as companion object properties (e.g., companion object { const val TYPE_HEADER = 0 })

rows.benefit: Only rebinds changed items — avoids full redraws on every update
Tells RecyclerView its size won't change — skips unnecessary layout passes
Eliminates repeated findViewById calls — references are cached at ViewHolder creation
Keep binding fast — pre-compute data transformations before passing to the adapter
Increases the off-screen view cache to reduce rebinding for recently scrolled items
Share ViewHolder pool between nested RecyclerViews of the same type to reduce inflation
Use Glide or Coil to load images off the main thread — never decode bitmaps in onBindViewHolder

sections.heading: Swipe to Dismiss & Drag to Reorder with ItemTouchHelper
items: ItemTouchHelper is a utility class that handles swipe-to-dismiss and drag-to-reorder gestures
Create an ItemTouchHelper.SimpleCallback(dragDirs, swipeDirs) subclass
dragDirs — combination of UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT for drag directions (0 to disable drag)
swipeDirs — LEFT and/or RIGHT for swipe directions (0 to disable swipe)
Override onMove(recyclerView, viewHolder, target) to handle drag-and-drop reordering
Override onSwiped(viewHolder, direction) to handle swipe actions (e.g., delete item)
Attach with: ItemTouchHelper(callback).attachToRecyclerView(recyclerView)
Update your data list and call adapter.notifyItemRemoved() or submitList() after swipe or move

rows.fix: Use ListAdapter with submitList() or granular notify methods for animated updates
Set them in onBindViewHolder so they capture the correct current item data

rows.mistake: Using position directly in async callbacks
rows.fix: Use holder.bindingAdapterPosition — position parameter can be stale after animations

rows.mistake: Calling setLayoutManager or setAdapter in onBindViewHolder of a nested RecyclerView
rows.fix: Only set LayoutManager once; check if it's already set before reassigning
areItemsTheSame must compare by ID, areContentsTheSame by all displayed fields

rows.mistake: Inflating complex layouts in onCreateViewHolder on the main thread
rows.fix: Keep item layouts simple; consider pre-inflation or AsyncLayoutInflater for heavy layouts

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: RecyclerView is the cornerstone of data-driven Android UIs. Its combination of mandatory view recycling, flexible LayoutManagers, and powerful DiffUtil-based updates makes it the right tool for displaying any list or grid of data. Always use ListAdapter with a proper DiffUtil.ItemCallback for clean, efficient, animated updates. Use View Binding in your ViewHolder for type-safe view access, and keep onBindViewHolder fast and lightweight for a smooth 60fps scrolling experience.
